1950.moment: India's field hockey team reigned as unbroken Olympic champions, having won every Olympic gold from Amsterdam 1928 through London 1948 and riding an unbeaten major-tournament streak that would stretch on to a sixth straight gold at Helsinki 1952.

1951.moment: India hosted the first-ever Asian Games in New Delhi and finished 2nd with 15 golds. Swimmer Sachin Nag won India's first Asian Games gold (100m freestyle), the football team beat Iran to take gold, and sprinter Lavy Pinto won both the 100m and 200m.

1952.moment: At the Helsinki Olympics, wrestler Khashaba Dadasaheb (K.D.) Jadhav won bronze in bantamweight freestyle, becoming independent India's first individual Olympic medallist, as the hockey team simultaneously claimed its fifth straight Olympic gold.

1953.moment: India remained the world's dominant hockey nation, reigning Olympic champions after Helsinki 1952 and unbeaten in Olympic play since 1928, the sport's golden age at its peak between Games.

1954.moment: India's footballers won gold at the Manila Asian Games, beating Iran in the final with a Neville D'Souza-era side led by striker Mewalal, the tournament's top scorer, adding to India's status as an early Asian football power.

1955.moment: India held its throne as Olympic hockey champions, the reigning gold medallists from Helsinki 1952 preparing to defend an unbeaten Olympic record stretching back to 1928 ahead of Melbourne 1956.

1956.moment: India won its sixth consecutive Olympic hockey gold at the Melbourne Games, beating arch-rivals Pakistan 1-0 in the final, captained by Balbir Singh Sr., who top-scored across the tournament.

1957.moment: Fresh off a sixth straight Olympic gold at Melbourne 1956, India remained the undisputed kings of world hockey, still unbeaten in Olympic competition since the sport's Indian dynasty began in 1928.

1958.moment: Milkha Singh won 440-yards gold at the Cardiff Commonwealth Games (independent India's first CWG gold) plus 200m and 400m golds at the Tokyo Asian Games; the same year Wilson Jones became India's first world champion in any sport, winning the World Amateur Billiards title in Calcutta.

1959.moment: Milkha Singh, the newly crowned Commonwealth and Asian Games sprint champion, reigned as India's track superstar, holding national records and building toward his celebrated 1960 Rome Olympic run.

1960.moment: At the Rome Olympics Milkha Singh finished a heartbreaking 4th in the 400m final in 45.6s, a national record that stood nearly 40 years; the same Games saw India's hockey team lose 0-1 to Pakistan in the final, ending its 30-year, six-gold Olympic reign with silver.

1961.moment: Milkha Singh remained India's premier athlete, the 'Flying Sikh' holding the 400m national record from his Rome run and preparing to reclaim continental titles at the upcoming Jakarta Asian Games.

1962.moment: At the Jakarta Asian Games, Milkha Singh won gold in both the 400m and the 4x400m relay, leading India to a strong 3rd-place finish with 10 golds and cementing his status as Asia's finest sprinter.

1963.moment: Billiards world champion Wilson Jones, India's first world titlist in any sport, was honoured with the Arjuna Award, reflecting his standing as the country's leading global sportsman between hockey's Olympic campaigns.

1964.moment: India reclaimed the Olympic hockey crown at the Tokyo Games, avenging its 1960 loss by beating Pakistan 1-0 in the final for a seventh gold; Wilson Jones also won a second World Amateur Billiards title that year.

1965.moment: India stood as reigning Olympic hockey champions after Tokyo 1964, having beaten Pakistan for the gold, with the sport still India's foremost source of world sporting glory.

1966.moment: India won its first-ever Asian Games hockey gold at the Bangkok Asian Games, defeating Pakistan in the final and finally topping the continental podium in its national sport.

1967.moment: India held both the Olympic hockey title (Tokyo 1964) and the Asian Games hockey gold (Bangkok 1966), a rare simultaneous double at the top of world and continental hockey.

1968.moment: At the Mexico City Olympics India took bronze in hockey, beaten by Australia in the semi-final before defeating West Germany for third, the first time India failed to reach an Olympic hockey final.

1969.moment: India remained a leading world hockey power as reigning Asian Games champions, regrouping after its Mexico 1968 Olympic bronze ahead of the 1970 Bangkok Asian Games.

1970.moment: At the Bangkok Asian Games, Kamaljeet Sandhu won the women's 400m in 57.3s to become the first Indian woman to win an individual gold at the Asian Games, a landmark for Indian women's sport.

1971.moment: Kamaljeet Sandhu, the first Indian woman to win Asian Games individual gold (400m, Bangkok 1970), was awarded the Padma Shri, honouring a breakthrough moment for Indian women in athletics.

1972.moment: India won hockey bronze at the Munich Olympics, losing to Pakistan in the semi-final and then beating the Netherlands (via a late Mukhbain Singh goal) for third place.

1973.moment: India took silver at the inaugural-era Hockey World Cup runs, finishing runner-up at the 1973 World Cup in Amsterdam after losing the final to the Netherlands in a penalty shootout.

1974.moment: India won silver at the Tehran Asian Games hockey tournament, finishing behind Pakistan, as it built the core of the side that would triumph at the World Cup the following year.

1975.moment: India won the Hockey World Cup in Kuala Lumpur, its only World Cup title, with captain Ajit Pal Singh and a decisive goal from Ashok Kumar in a 2-1 final win over arch-rivals Pakistan.

1976.moment: India, the reigning 1975 World Cup champions, competed at the Montreal Olympics, the first Games played on artificial turf, a surface shift that would reshape the balance of world hockey.

1977.moment: India remained the reigning hockey World Cup holders from its 1975 Kuala Lumpur triumph, the last great high of Indian hockey's classic era before the 1980 Moscow Olympic gold.

1978.moment: India collected 11 golds at the Bangkok Asian Games, its strongest gold haul since 1951, powered by wins across athletics, kabaddi and other disciplines as the country broadened its medal base.

1979.moment: India, still the 1975 World Cup holders, prepared its squad for the Moscow Olympics, where a depleted field would give India its chance to reclaim Olympic hockey gold.

1980.moment: India won its eighth and most recent Olympic hockey gold at the Moscow Games, beating Spain 4-3 in the final, the last time India topped the Olympic hockey podium.

1981.moment: A young P.T. Usha emerged as India's next track sensation, setting national records at a senior inter-country meet in Bangalore and signalling the arrival of the athlete who would dominate Asian sprinting.

1982.moment: At the New Delhi Asian Games, 18-year-old P.T. Usha announced herself by winning silver medals in both the 100m (11.67s) and 200m (24.32s), launching a golden era for Indian women's athletics.

1983.moment: Prakash Padukone, at the peak of his powers after his 1980 All England title, won bronze at the 1983 World Badminton Championships in Copenhagen — a rare global podium for Indian badminton in that era.

1984.moment: PT Usha finished 4th in the 400m hurdles final at the Los Angeles Olympics in 55.42 seconds, missing the bronze medal by just 1/100th of a second — the closest an Indian had come to an Olympic track medal.

1985.moment: PT Usha dominated the Asian Athletics Championships in Jakarta, winning five golds (100m, 200m, 400m, 400m hurdles, 4x400m relay) and a bronze — a record haul at a single edition of the championships.

1986.moment: At the Seoul Asian Games, PT Usha won four golds (200m, 400m, 400m hurdles, 4x400m relay) and a silver (100m), setting Games records in every gold-medal event and cementing her as the 'Payyoli Express'.

1987.moment: Viswanathan Anand became the first Indian to win the World Junior Chess Championship, and later that year clinched India's first Grandmaster title at age 18 in Coimbatore.

1988.moment: Viswanathan Anand was confirmed as India's first-ever chess Grandmaster, opening the door for a generation of Indian players and beginning his rise into the global elite.

1989.moment: Dhanraj Pillay made his debut for the Indian men's hockey team, injecting new energy into the sport as India rebuilt after a lean 1980s decade of Asian Games bronzes and near-misses.

1990.moment: Leander Paes, aged 17, won the Wimbledon junior singles title and rose to World No. 1 in the junior rankings, announcing himself as India's next great tennis talent.

1991.moment: Leander Paes captured the US Open junior singles title and turned professional, backing up his 1990 Wimbledon junior crown and launching his senior career.

1992.moment: Leander Paes and Ramesh Krishnan reached the doubles quarterfinals at the Barcelona Olympics, the best Indian tennis result at the Games at the time.

1993.moment: Leander Paes helped India reach the Davis Cup semifinals, beating Switzerland and France before falling to Australia — a rare deep run for Indian tennis on the world stage.

1994.moment: At the Hiroshima Asian Games, Leander Paes won singles bronze plus doubles and team golds, dominating the continental tennis events.

1995.moment: Viswanathan Anand challenged Garry Kasparov for the PCA World Chess Championship at the World Trade Center in New York, opening with a record eight straight draws before losing 10.5-7.5 — the closest an Indian had come to the world title.

1996.moment: Leander Paes won the men's singles bronze at the Atlanta Olympics, India's first individual Olympic medal since 1952 and its first Olympic medal in 16 years.

1997.moment: Viswanathan Anand consolidated his standing among the world's best, winning elite events and climbing toward a FIDE world-title shot as India's undisputed sporting icon in chess.

1998.moment: India won hockey gold at the Bangkok Asian Games, ending a 32-year continental title drought by beating South Korea, powered by a stellar campaign from Dhanraj Pillay.

1999.moment: The doubles pair of Leander Paes and Mahesh Bhupathi reached all four Grand Slam finals in a single year, winning the French Open and Wimbledon to become the world's top-ranked doubles team.

2000.moment: Karnam Malleswari won bronze in the 69kg category at the Sydney Olympics, becoming the first Indian woman ever to win an Olympic medal. The same year, Anand won his first FIDE World Chess Championship.

2001.moment: Viswanathan Anand, reigning FIDE world champion, continued his dominance on the international circuit, remaining India's premier world-beating athlete in an otherwise quiet year for Indian sport.

2002.moment: Anju Bobby George won long jump gold at the Busan Asian Games (after a Commonwealth Games bronze in Manchester), emerging as India's finest jumper.

2003.moment: Anju Bobby George won long jump bronze at the World Athletics Championships in Paris — India's first-ever medal at a World Athletics Championships. Pankaj Advani also won a world snooker title this year.

2004.moment: Rajyavardhan Singh Rathore won silver in men's double trap at the Athens Olympics, India's first individual Olympic silver and a landmark for Indian shooting.

2005.moment: Pankaj Advani achieved the 'Grand Double,' winning both the point and time formats of the IBSF World Billiards Championship in the same year — the first player ever to do so.

2006.moment: A 16-year-old Saina Nehwal won the Philippines Open to become the first Indian woman and youngest Asian to win a 4-star tournament, and reached the World Junior Championships final.

2007.moment: Viswanathan Anand won the World Chess Championship tournament in Mexico City with an undefeated performance, becoming the undisputed world champion.

2008.moment: Abhinav Bindra won gold in the 10m air rifle at the Beijing Olympics — India's first-ever individual Olympic gold. Wrestler Sushil Kumar and boxer Vijender Singh added bronzes at the same Games.

2009.moment: Saina Nehwal won the Indonesia Open, becoming the first Indian to win a BWF Super Series title, the sport's most prestigious tournament tier.

2010.moment: India hosted the Delhi Commonwealth Games and finished second with a best-ever 38 golds and 101 total medals. Wrestler Sushil Kumar also became India's first world wrestling champion in Moscow, and Saina Nehwal won Commonwealth Games gold.
